content:
# Acts Chapter 10 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 237-238 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_010_page_001.jpg, ACT_010_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now there was a certain man in Caesarea named Cornelius, a centurion of the cohort called the Italian. **2** He was devout and one who feared God with all his household, giving many alms to the people and praying to God continually. **3** He saw clearly in a vision, about the ninth hour of the day, an angel of God coming in to him and saying to him, "Cornelius." **4** And gazing at him and becoming afraid, he said, "What is it, Lord?" And he said to him, "Your prayers and your alms have ascended as a memorial before God. **5** And now send men to Joppa and summon a certain Simon who is called Peter. **6** This one is lodging with a certain Simon, a tanner, whose house is by the sea."

**7** And when the angel who spoke to him had departed, he called two of his household servants and a devout soldier from among those who attended him constantly. **8** And having explained everything to them, he sent them to Joppa.

**9** And on the next day, as they were journeying and drawing near to the city, Peter went up on the housetop to pray, about the sixth hour. **10** And he became hungry and desired to eat. But while they were preparing, a trance fell upon him. **11** And he beheld heaven opened, and a certain vessel descending like a great sheet, being let down by four corners upon the earth, **12** in which were all the four-footed animals and creeping things of the earth and birds of heaven. **13** And a voice came to him, "Rise, Peter, kill and eat." **14** But Peter said, "By no means, Lord, for I have never eaten anything common or unclean." **15** And a voice came to him again a second time, "What God has cleansed, you must not call common." **16** And this happened three times, and immediately the vessel was taken up into heaven.

**17** And while Peter was greatly perplexed within himself as to what the vision that he had seen might mean, behold, the men who had been sent by Cornelius, having inquired for the house of Simon, stood at the gate. **18** And calling out, they asked whether Simon who is called Peter was lodging there. **19** And while Peter was pondering the vision, the Spirit said to him, "Behold, men are seeking you. **20** But rise, go down, and go with them, doubting nothing, for I have sent them." **21** And Peter went down to the men and said, "Behold, I am the one whom you are seeking. What is the reason for which you have come?" **22** And they said, "Cornelius, a centurion, a righteous man and one who fears God, well spoken of by the whole nation of the Jews, was directed by a holy angel to summon you to his house and to hear words from you."

**23** So he invited them in and gave them lodging. And on the next day he arose and went out with them, and some of the brothers from Joppa accompanied him. **24** And on the following day they entered Caesarea. And Cornelius was expecting them, having called together his relatives and close friends. **25** And as it came to pass that Peter entered, Cornelius met him and fell at his feet and worshiped him. **26** But Peter raised him up, saying, "Stand up; I myself also am a man." **27** And conversing with him, he went in and found many who had come together. **28** And he said to them, "You yourselves know that it is unlawful for a Jewish man to associate with or to visit one of another nation. Yet God has shown me that I should not call any person common or unclean. **29** Therefore also I came without objection when I was sent for. So I ask, for what reason did you send for me?"

**30** And Cornelius said, "Four days ago, until this hour, I was fasting and praying at the ninth hour in my house, and behold, a man stood before me in bright clothing **31** and said, 'Cornelius, your prayer has been heard and your alms have been remembered before God. **32** Send therefore to Joppa and summon Simon who is called Peter. This one is lodging in the house of Simon, a tanner, by the sea.' **33** So I sent to you at once, and you have done well in coming. Now therefore we are all present before God to hear all the things that have been commanded you by the Lord."

**34** And Peter opened his mouth and said, "In truth I perceive that God is not one who shows partiality, **35** but in every nation the one who fears him and works righteousness is acceptable to him. **36** The word which he sent to the sons of Israel, proclaiming the good news of peace through Jesus Christ -- this one is Lord of all -- **37** you yourselves know the thing that happened throughout all Judea, beginning from Galilee after the baptism that John proclaimed: **38** Jesus from Nazareth, how God anointed him with the Holy Spirit and with power, who went about doing good and healing all who were oppressed by the devil, for God was with him. **39** And we are witnesses of all things that he did both in the land of the Jews and in Jerusalem -- whom also they killed, hanging him on a tree. **40** This one God raised on the third day and granted that he should become manifest, **41** not to all the people, but to witnesses who had been chosen beforehand by God, to us who ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead. **42** And he commanded us to proclaim to the people and to testify solemnly that this is the one who has been appointed by God as judge of the living and the dead. **43** To him all the prophets bear witness, that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name."

**44** While Peter was still speaking these words, the Holy Spirit fell upon all those who were hearing the word. **45** And the believers from among the circumcision, as many as had come with Peter, were astonished, because the gift of the Holy Spirit had been poured out also upon the Gentiles. **46** For they were hearing them speaking in tongues and magnifying God. Then Peter answered, **47** "Can anyone withhold the water so that these should not be baptized, who have received the Holy Spirit just as we also did?" **48** And he commanded them to be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ. Then they asked him to remain for some days.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 10 is one of the most consequential chapters in the entire New Testament. It narrates the conversion of Cornelius, a Roman centurion and God-fearer (vv. 1-8), Peter's vision of the sheet and the divine abolition of the clean/unclean distinction (vv. 9-16), the arrival of Cornelius's messengers and the Spirit's directive (vv. 17-23a), Peter's journey to Caesarea and his sermon in Cornelius's house (vv. 23b-43), and the outpouring of the Holy Spirit on the Gentiles with their subsequent baptism (vv. 44-48). This chapter marks the decisive turning point in Acts: the barrier between Jew and Gentile is shattered by direct divine action, and the universal scope of the gospel is irrevocably established.

**Manuscript condition:** The text begins on page 237 (folio 118v), where Acts 9 concludes in the first column and Acts 10 begins in the second column (the column heading at the bottom of the page reads "Acts 10:6"). The chapter continues across all four columns of page 237 and the first three columns of page 238 (folio 119r), concluding before Acts 11 begins in the fourth column. The manuscript is well-preserved and legible. Standard nomina sacra are used throughout (IHU XPU for Jesus Christ, KU for Lord, THU for God, PNA for Spirit). Several correction marks by later hands are visible but do not alter the primary readings followed here.

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show strong overall agreement on the chapter's narrative structure and theological content. The primary differences are stylistic: EMET preserves Greek syntax with participial chains and hyphenated compounds; LOGOS maintains formal equivalence with natural English phrasing; PNEUMA renders dynamically for contemporary accessibility. On several key textual matters -- the omission of "wild beasts" in v. 12, the omission of the Byzantine addition in v. 6, the reading "by the Lord" in v. 33 -- there is broad agreement.

**General translation approach:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS most closely in this chapter, as its formal equivalence balances fidelity to the Greek with English readability. EMET's contributions are drawn upon where they preserve important Greek features (e.g., the demonstrative "this one" in vv. 6, 36, 40, and the preservation of "common" vs. "unclean" as distinct terms). PNEUMA's dynamic clarity is consulted but rarely adopted, as this chapter's theological precision demands close adherence to the Greek terminology.

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **Verse 6 -- Omission of "this one will tell you what you must do":** All three translators correctly note that Sinaiticus omits the Byzantine addition found in later manuscripts. The angel's speech ends simply with "whose house is by the sea." SOPHIA follows the Sinaiticus text.

2. **Verse 11 -- "vessel" (skeuos) and "sheet" (othone):** EMET renders "linen-sheet"; LOGOS renders "vessel... like a great sheet"; PNEUMA renders "something like a great sheet." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "vessel" preserves the Greek skeuos as the primary noun, while "like a great sheet" translates the comparative description. The word othone refers to a linen cloth or sheet, and the image is of a large piece of fabric being lowered by its four corners.

3. **Verse 12 -- Omission of "wild beasts" (kai theria):** Sinaiticus omits "and wild beasts," listing only three categories: four-footed animals, creeping things, and birds. EMET and LOGOS correctly follow the Sinaiticus reading. PNEUMA also omits wild beasts but generalizes to "all kinds of." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in preserving the three-category list as found in the manuscript.

4. **Verse 14 -- "common or unclean" (koinon e akatharton):** This is a critical theological distinction. EMET and LOGOS both preserve the two terms: "common" (koinon -- ritually profane, accessible to ordinary use) and "unclean" (akatharton -- ceremonially impure). PNEUMA renders both as "impure or unclean," collapsing the distinction. SOPHIA preserves both terms with EMET and LOGOS. The distinction matters because Peter's vision addresses two separate categories of ritual exclusion.

5. **Verse 15 -- "you must not call common" (sy me koinou):** The verb koinou means "to make common" or "to declare common/profane." EMET renders "you must not make common"; LOGOS renders "you must not call common"; PNEUMA renders "you must not call impure." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "call common" captures the declarative force of the verb while preserving the key term koinon. PNEUMA's "impure" loses the specific theological vocabulary.

6. **Verse 19 -- Number of men:** This is a significant textual variant. EMET correctly notes that Sinaiticus reads simply ANDRES ("men") without specifying a number. LOGOS includes "three men," as does PNEUMA. Examining the manuscript image on page 237, the reading in Sinaiticus for this verse is contested -- the original hand appears to lack the numeral, though some scholars read a correction. SOPHIA follows the reading without a specified number ("men are seeking you"), as this is the more conservative rendering of the Sinaiticus text and aligns with EMET's careful observation. The context (two servants and one soldier in v. 7) makes three men narratively clear without requiring the number in the text.

7. **Verse 25 -- "worshiped" (prosekynesen):** All three translators use strong language: EMET has "worshiped"; LOGOS has "worshiped"; PNEUMA has "fell at his feet to worship him." SOPHIA retains "worshiped" -- Peter's sharp correction in v. 26 ("I myself also am a man") indicates Cornelius's action went beyond simple reverence. The verb proskyneo carries its full force here.

8. **Verse 28 -- "one of another nation" (allophylo):** EMET renders "one of-another-nation"; LOGOS renders "one of another nation"; PNEUMA renders "a Gentile." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Greek allophylos is more precise than "Gentile" (ethnos) -- it means "of another tribe/race" and carries the specific resonance of foreignness rather than the broader theological category.

9. **Verse 30 -- "fasting and" (nesteuon kai):** LOGOS notes that Sinaiticus includes the fasting reference (NESTEUON). EMET omits it; PNEUMA omits it. Examining the manuscript, LOGOS appears correct that Sinaiticus preserves a longer reading including fasting. SOPHIA includes "fasting and praying" following the Sinaiticus text as LOGOS reports it.

10. **Verse 33 -- "by the Lord" (hypo tou kyriou) vs. "by God" (hypo tou theou):** EMET correctly notes Sinaiticus reads "by the Lord" rather than "by God." LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "the Lord." SOPHIA follows the Sinaiticus reading: "by the Lord." This is a significant variant -- "the Lord" here could refer to Christ, giving the passage a higher Christological weight.

11. **Verse 34 -- "God is not one who shows partiality" (prosopolemptes):** EMET renders "God is not a respecter-of-persons"; LOGOS renders "God is not one who shows partiality"; PNEUMA renders "God does not show favoritism." The Greek prosopolemptes literally means "face-receiver" -- one who judges by outward appearance or status. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "not one who shows partiality" preserves the substantive form (prosopolemptes is a noun, not a verb) while communicating clearly. EMET's "respecter-of-persons" is archaic; PNEUMA's "favoritism" is clear but loses the formal register.

12. **Verses 36-37 -- Anacoluthon:** The Greek syntax of vv. 36-37 is notoriously difficult. The sentence begins with "the word which he sent" (v. 36) but shifts construction midway, with "you yourselves know the thing that happened" (v. 37) picking up an entirely new grammatical thread. This is an anacoluthon -- a broken sentence structure that may reflect Peter's passionate oral delivery. EMET preserves the roughness faithfully. LOGOS handles it with a colon after the parenthetical. PNEUMA smooths it substantially. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's approach: preserving the parenthetical "this one is Lord of all" with double-dashes and allowing the syntactic break between v. 36 and v. 37 to stand, with a colon marking the transition. This preserves the oral, unpolished character of Peter's speech without rendering it unintelligible.

13. **Verse 36 -- "this one is Lord of all" (houtos estin panton kyrios):** All three translators preserve this parenthetical declaration. SOPHIA sets it off with double-dashes, consistent with the convention used throughout the SOPHIA Bible. This is one of the most theologically significant statements in Acts -- Peter declares the Jewish Messiah as universal Lord while standing in a Gentile household.

14. **Verse 39 -- "hanging him on a tree" (kremasantes epi xylou):** EMET and LOGOS render "tree" (xylon); PNEUMA renders "cross." SOPHIA retains "tree" -- xylon means "wood" or "tree" and deliberately echoes Deuteronomy 21:22-23 (LXX), where hanging on a tree signifies being under God's curse. This allusion is theologically essential and would be lost by rendering it "cross."

15. **Verse 45 -- "the believers from among the circumcision" (hoi pistoi ek peritomes):** EMET renders "the faithful ones of the circumcision"; LOGOS renders "the believers from among the circumcision"; PNEUMA renders "the Jewish believers." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The phrase identifies these men specifically as Jewish Christians who accompanied Peter, and the formal rendering preserves Luke's careful sociological language.

16. **Verse 47 -- Rhetorical question:** EMET renders literally: "Surely no one is able to forbid the water..."; LOGOS renders: "Can anyone withhold the water..."; PNEUMA renders: "Can anyone withhold the water for baptizing these people?" SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Greek construction (me ti... dynatai kolysai tis) expects a negative answer. The rhetorical force is clear: the Spirit has already acted; water baptism is now the only appropriate response.

17. **Verse 48 -- "in the name of Jesus Christ":** Sinaiticus reads EN TO ONOMATI IESOU CHRISTOU with characteristic nomina sacra (IHU XPU). Some manuscripts read "in the name of the Lord" or "in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ." All three translators follow the Sinaiticus reading. SOPHIA concurs.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Opening syntax | "And a certain man in Caesarea" | "Now there was a certain man in Caesarea" | "In Caesarea there was a man" | "Now there was a certain man in Caesarea" -- follows LOGOS; preserves tis (certain) and smooth transition |
| 2 | "alms" vs. "acts of charity" | "doing many alms to the people" | "who gave many alms to the people" | "gave generously to those in need" | "giving many alms to the people" -- preserves eleemosynas (alms); PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the technical term |
| 3 | "ninth hour" vs. "three o'clock" | "the ninth hour of the day" | "the ninth hour of the day" | "around three o'clock" | "the ninth hour of the day" -- 2-to-1; preserves the Jewish time-reckoning of the source |
| 4 | "ascended" vs. "gone up" | "gone up for a memorial" | "ascended as a memorial" | "risen before God as an offering he remembers" | "ascended as a memorial" -- follows LOGOS; "ascended" carries sacrificial overtones; PNEUMA over-interprets mnemosynon |
| 10 | "trance" vs. "ecstasy" | "an ecstasy fell upon him" | "a trance fell upon him" | "he fell into a trance" | "a trance fell upon him" -- 2-to-1; Greek ekstasis means standing-outside-oneself; "trance" is the standard English equivalent |
| 11 | "vessel" vs. "something like" | "a certain vessel descending, as a great linen-sheet" | "a certain vessel descending like a great sheet" | "something like a great sheet being lowered" | "a certain vessel descending like a great sheet" -- follows LOGOS; preserves skeuos as the noun with othone as the comparative |
| 12 | "wild beasts" included? | Omitted (three categories) | Omitted (three categories) | Omitted (three categories) | Omitted -- Sinaiticus reads three categories only; all three translators agree |
| 14 | "common or unclean" vs. "impure or unclean" | "common and unclean" | "common or unclean" | "impure or unclean" | "common or unclean" -- follows LOGOS; koinon and akatharton are distinct categories; PNEUMA collapses the distinction |
| 15 | "make common" vs. "call common" vs. "call impure" | "you must not make common" | "you must not call common" | "you must not call impure" | "you must not call common" -- follows LOGOS; "call" captures the declarative force; "common" preserves koinou |
| 19 | Number of men | "men" (no number) | "three men" | "three men" | "men" (no number) -- follows EMET; Sinaiticus original hand lacks the numeral; the more conservative reading |
| 21 | "Behold, I am the one" vs. "I am the one" | "Behold, I am the one whom you are seeking" | "Behold, I am the one whom you are seeking" | "I am the one you are looking for" | "Behold, I am the one whom you are seeking" -- 2-to-1; preserves idou and the formal relative clause |
| 22 | "testified-to" vs. "well spoken of" vs. "respected" | "being testified-to by" | "well spoken of by" | "respected by" | "well spoken of by" -- follows LOGOS; martyroumenon means "attested/testified about"; LOGOS captures the sense naturally |
| 25 | "worshiped" rendering | "worshiped" | "worshiped" | "fell at his feet to worship him" | "worshiped" -- all three agree on proskyneo; SOPHIA preserves the simple aorist |
| 28 | "of another nation" vs. "Gentile" | "one of-another-nation" | "one of another nation" | "a Gentile" | "one of another nation" -- follows LOGOS; allophylos is more specific than ethnos; PNEUMA's "Gentile" is too broad |
| 28 | "common or unclean" (of persons) | "common or unclean" | "common or unclean" | "impure or unclean" | "common or unclean" -- 2-to-1; same distinction as v. 14 now applied to persons |
| 30 | "fasting and praying" included? | Omitted | Included (fasting) | Omitted | Included -- Sinaiticus appears to include NESTEUON; SOPHIA follows the manuscript with LOGOS |
| 30 | "bright clothing" vs. "dazzling clothes" | "bright clothing" | "bright clothing" | "dazzling clothes" | "bright clothing" -- 2-to-1; lampra = shining/bright; "dazzling" over-intensifies |
| 33 | "by the Lord" vs. "by God" | "by the Lord" (hypo tou kyriou) | "by the Lord" | "the Lord has commanded" | "by the Lord" -- Sinaiticus reads kyriou; all three follow this reading |
| 34 | "respecter-of-persons" vs. "shows partiality" vs. "favoritism" | "God is not a respecter-of-persons" | "God is not one who shows partiality" | "God does not show favoritism" | "God is not one who shows partiality" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the noun prosopolemptes; EMET is archaic, PNEUMA too informal |
| 36-37 | Anacoluthon handling | Preserves rough syntax literally | Colon and parenthetical with dashes | Smoothed into natural English | Follows LOGOS approach with double-dashes; preserves the syntactic break as a feature of Peter's oral delivery |
| 36 | "sons of Israel" vs. "people of Israel" | "sons of Israel" | "sons of Israel" | "people of Israel" | "sons of Israel" -- 2-to-1; huiois Israēl is literally "sons of Israel" |
| 39 | "tree" vs. "cross" | "tree" (xylon) | "tree" | "cross" | "tree" -- 2-to-1; xylon means wood/tree; preserves the Deuteronomy 21:22-23 allusion |
| 40 | "gave him to become manifest" vs. "granted" | "gave him to become manifest" | "granted that he should become manifest" | "made him visible" | "granted that he should become manifest" -- follows LOGOS; balances EMET's literalism with readability |
| 41 | "chosen beforehand" vs. "chosen by God beforehand" | "witnesses having been chosen-beforehand by God" | "witnesses who had been chosen beforehand by God" | "witnesses chosen by God beforehand" | "witnesses who had been chosen beforehand by God" -- follows LOGOS; preserves procheirotoneō in proper English syntax |
| 45 | "believers from among the circumcision" | "faithful ones of the circumcision" | "believers from among the circumcision" | "Jewish believers" | "believers from among the circumcision" -- follows LOGOS; preserves Luke's sociological precision; PNEUMA simplifies excessively |
| 45 | "astonished" vs. "stunned" | "were astonished" | "were astonished" | "were stunned" | "were astonished" -- 2-to-1; exestēsan; both are valid but "astonished" matches the formal register |
| 47 | Rhetorical question phrasing | "Surely no one is able to forbid the water" | "Can anyone withhold the water" | "Can anyone withhold the water for baptizing these people?" | "Can anyone withhold the water so that these should not be baptized" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the mē construction |
| 48 | "some days" vs. "a few days" | "some days" | "some days" | "a few days" | "some days" -- 2-to-1; hēmeras tinas is indefinite; "a few" implies a small number not in the Greek |

-- SOPHIA
